myth: Voters directly elect the Prime Minister of India.
fact: Citizens elect their local Member of Parliament (MP). The party or alliance with a majority in the Lok Sabha then chooses their leader to be appointed as PM by the President.

myth: All Indian elections (National, State, Local) happen on the same day.
fact: No, they usually happen separately based on when the 5-year term of that specific body ends. There are discussions about 'One Nation, One Election', but it's not current law.

myth: Missing one election removes you from the voter list forever.
fact: Your name remains on the Electoral Roll unless you move, die, or are disqualified. You can vote in the next election as long as you are registered.

myth: If NOTA wins, the election is cancelled and re-held.
fact: Under current rules, even if NOTA gets the highest votes, the candidate with the next highest number of votes is declared the winner. NOTA is for expressing dissent.

myth: Rajya Sabha members are elected directly by the people.
fact: Rajya Sabha members are elected indirectly by the elected members of State Legislative Assemblies (MLAs). 12 members are nominated by the President.

myth: EVMs can be hacked via WiFi or Bluetooth.
fact: EVMs are standalone machines with no wireless communication hardware. They cannot be networked and have no internet connectivity.

myth: The richest candidate always wins the election.
fact: While money can influence campaigning, many candidates with fewer resources have won based on their work and popularity. ECI also enforces spending limits.

myth: Voting is compulsory by law in India.
fact: Voting is a fundamental right and a civic duty, but it is not legally compulsory. There is no fine for not voting, though everyone is encouraged to participate.

myth: MP and MLA mean the same thing.
fact: MPs work in the national Parliament, while MLAs work in the State Legislative Assembly. They have different roles and different constituencies.

myth: The party with the most total votes always forms the government.
fact: In India's FPTP system, the party with the most SEATS forms the government, not necessarily the one with the highest percentage of total votes polled.

myth: The President of India runs the daily government.
fact: The President is the constitutional head, but the daily administration is run by the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister.

myth: The Prime Minister appoints the Chief Ministers of states.
fact: The Governor of each state appoints the CM based on the majority in that state's Vidhan Sabha. The PM has no constitutional role in this.

myth: You absolutely need a physical Voter ID card to vote.
fact: If your name is in the Electoral Roll, you can vote by showing any of the 12 approved documents like Aadhaar, Passport, or PAN card.

myth: ECI is a department under the central government.
fact: The Election Commission of India is an independent constitutional body. It is not controlled by any ministry and its independence is protected by law.

myth: Only educated people or taxpayers can vote in India.
fact: India follows 'Universal Adult Franchise'. Every citizen aged 18 or above has an equal right to vote, regardless of education, wealth, or gender.

myth: A by-election means the previous election was illegal.
fact: By-elections happen when a seat becomes vacant due to the death or resignation of a member. It doesn't mean the original election was invalid.

myth: You can vote in any booth as long as you have a Voter ID.
fact: You can ONLY vote at the specific polling station where your name is registered in the Electoral Roll.

myth: Silent period means no political posts on social media by anyone.
fact: The law prohibits public meetings and media ads by candidates/parties. While personal posts are complex to regulate, major platforms adhere to ECI guidelines.
